1: 'At this my heart trembles (yehĕrad libbî) and leaps from its place' — Elihu's personal response to the theophanic signs he is describing. The verb ḥāraḏ (to tremble/quake with awe) is the standard theophanic-approach response: Exodus 19:16 (people trembled at Sinai); 1 Samuel 4:13 (Eli trembles at bad news); Isaiah 66:2 ('to this one I will look: to him who is humble and contrite in spirit, who trembles [ḥāreḏ] at my word'). The heart leaping out of its place is a more extreme image — the internal organ of discernment is destabilized by divine approach.
2: 'Listen carefully (šimʿû šāmôaʿ) to the roar of his voice (bĕrōgez qōlô) and the rumble that goes out from his mouth' — the double imperative (infinitive absolute + finite verb = intensive command) focuses attention on the divine voice. rōgez (normally 'agitation/anger') here describes the thunderous quality of the divine voice — not wrathful anger but the disturbing power of the sound. The double auditory reference (voice/qōl + rumble going forth) parallels Ps 29's 'voice of the LORD' (qôl-YHWH) thunderstorm poem, which uses the same term seven times.
3: 'He sends it (lightning) under the whole sky (taḥat kol-haššāmayim) and his lightning (ʾôrô) to the ends of the earth' — the global scope of divine lightning establishes that what Elihu describes is not a local weather event but a theophanic sign addressed to the whole earth. The 'ends of the earth' (ḵanp̄ôṯ hāʾāreṣ = the wings/extremities of the land) appears in Isaiah 40:28; Job 41:5,9; 43:6 as the reaches of YHWH's sovereign domain. The same formula in Psalms 97:4 follows theophanic lightning language: 'his lightnings lit up the world; the earth saw and trembled.'
4: 'After the lightning a voice roars (yiṣʿaq); he thunders (yarʿēm) in his majestic voice (bĕqôl gĕʾônô)' — the sequential meteorological theology: lightning precedes thunder (as observed), but in theological terms the voice (thunder) is the meaningful communication while the lightning is the sign that precedes it. gĕʾôn (majesty/pride) when applied to God is positive transcendence: Psalms 68:34 'his majesty is over Israel and his power in the clouds.' 'He does not hold the lightnings back once his voice sounds' — the completeness of divine disclosure: once God speaks, the full arsenal of theophanic signs follows without restraint.
5: 'God thunders astoundingly (p̄elāʾôṯ) with his voice' — p̄eleʾ (wonder/marvel) as a divine attribute: Psalms 77:11 'I will remember your wonders (p̄ilĕʾôṯeyḵā)'; Isaiah 9:6 (the Messianic title 'Wonderful Counselor', p̄eleh yôʿēṣ). 'He does great things beyond our understanding (wĕlōʾ nēḏāʿ)' — the same lōʾ-nēḏāʿ incomprehensibility predicate from 36:26 reappears: the theme of divine incomprehensibility brackets Elihu's meteorological hymn (Job 36:26; Job 37:5). This double framing prepares for YHWH's own incomprehensibility questions in Job 38.
6: 'For he says to the snow: Fall upon the earth' — the divine command (ʾāmar = he says) to snow as a personal instruction illustrates that natural phenomena are not impersonal forces but responsive servants of the divine word. This personalizes the cosmological relationship: the Psalmist makes the same point in Psalms 148:7-8 ('praise the LORD from the earth... snow and clouds, stormy wind fulfilling his word'). The parallel to Gen 1 is deliberate: there too God speaks and natural phenomena come into existence or behavior.
7: 'He seals (ḥātam) the hand of every person (bĕyaḏ-kol-ʾāḏām) so that all humanity may perceive (lāḏaʿaṯ) his work' — ḥātam (to seal/mark) the human hand: this is difficult, and interpretations divide. Most likely: during winter storms, fieldwork is impossible ('sealed off') so human beings are forced to stop their activity and observe the divine works. The theophanic storm functions pedagogically — it creates the attentional space for perceiving God's activity. An alternative reading sees the seal as a marking/signing, analogous to Job's own legal 'signature' in Job 31:35 (tāw = Job's mark).
8: 'Then the wild animals go into their dens and stay in their lairs' — the animal response to the divine storm: ordered retreat to shelter rather than chaos. This image of instinctual creaturely response to weather will be picked up in Job 38-39's survey of wild animals, where YHWH points to animal behavior as evidence of providential governance beyond human comprehension or control. The animals know their place in the divine order; the implicit critique of Job is that he does not similarly recognize his.
9: 'From the south (mĕḥēḏer, = inner room/chamber) comes the whirlwind (sûp̄āh)' — the 'chamber' of the south where the whirlwind originates. sûp̄āh (whirlwind/storm) here anticipates the divine answer in 38:1 from sĕʿārāh (whirlwind/storm). Both terms describe the theophanic storm; Elihu's meteorological hymn approaches the same storm form that YHWH will actually speak from. 'And from the north comes the cold (qārāh)' — the two directional extremes (south whirlwind, north cold) frame the complete meteorological range under divine governance.
10: 'By the breath of God (mĕnišmaṯ-ʾēl) ice is formed and the wide waters are frozen solid' — nišmaṯ-ʾēl (breath of God) echoes the creation vocabulary of Genesis 2:7 (nišmaṯ ḥayyîm = breath of life) and Job 32:8 (Elihu's own earlier claim that 'the breath of the Almighty gives understanding'). Here the divine breath is meteorological rather than animating — it freezes rather than vivifies. The double use of nišmāh suggests Elihu sees continuity between the creation breath and the ongoing meteorological activity: both are expressions of the same divine respiratory action.
11: 'He loads the clouds with moisture (bĕrî yāṭrîaḥ ʿāḇ) and scatters his lightning-cloud' — the clouds as weighted vessels that God fills and deploys. The physics are accurate: clouds are literally 'loaded' with water. Theologically, the meteorological process is a divine logistics operation: collection, loading, transport, and deployment of water resources. The image of God 'scattering' (yāp̄îṣ) the lightning cloud connects to the Psalms' imagery of divine arrows scattered against the enemy (Psalms 18:14; Job 144:6).
12: 'It turns in every direction by his guidance (bĕtaḥbûlōṯāyw), to do whatever he commands across the face of the inhabited earth' — taḥbûlāh (guidance/steersmanship; from ḥeḇel = rope, specifically a ship's steering rope). The metaphor is nautical: God is the helmsman of meteorological phenomena. The word appears in Proverbs 1:5; Job 11:14; Job 12:5; Job 20:18; 24:6 for the skilled navigation of human affairs (military strategy, wise counsel). By using ship-steering vocabulary for weather governance, Elihu frames divine providence as expert navigational skill rather than arbitrary force.
13: 'He brings it whether as discipline (lĕšēbeṭ), for his land's sake (lĕʾarṣô), or as steadfast love (lĕḥeseḏ)' — this tripartite purpose for storms is unique to Elihu and theologically significant: the same meteorological event serves three potentially different divine intentions depending on who receives it. šēbeṭ (rod/staff/discipline) is the discipline of Proverbs 13:24 ('he who hates his son spares the rod') and Hebrews 12:5-11; lĕʾarṣô (for his land) is providential care for creation's welfare; ḥeseḏ (steadfast covenant love) signals that weather itself can be an expression of covenantal grace.
14: 'Listen to this, Job; stop (ʿămōḏ = stand still) and consider (hiṯbônān) the wondrous works of God' — the direct address to Job after the impersonal meteorological description. The imperative to stand still recalls Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:5 'take off your sandals') and Israel at the sea (Exodus 14:13 'Stand firm and see the salvation of the LORD'). hiṯbônān (hitpa'el of bîn = to discern/understand) is intensive reflection: not passive observation but active contemplation. The wondrous works (nip̄lĕʾôṯ ʾēl) echo the Exodus miracles of Psalms 78:4 and connect to Isaiah 28:29 ('his works are wonderful; his counsel is magnificent').
15: 'Do you know how God arranged (śîm) these things and caused the lightning in his cloud to flash?' — the first rhetorical question preparing the mode of Job 38's cosmogonic interrogation. śîm (to set/arrange/place) is the creator's organizing action: God arranges the meteorological elements. The lightning 'flashing' (hôpîaʿ, from yāp̄aʿ = to shine out) is the same term used for divine epiphany in Psalms 80:1 ('you who sit enthroned between the cherubim, shine forth') and Job 94:1 ('God... shine forth'). The meteorological and theophanic vocabularies overlap intentionally.
16: 'Do you understand how the clouds are balanced (mip̄lĕśê ʿāḇ) — the wonders of the one who is perfect in knowledge (tĕmîm dēʿôṯ)?' — mip̄lĕśê (balancing/equipoising, from p̄ālas = to weigh) applied to clouds: how are they suspended rather than falling? This was a genuine ancient puzzle — what keeps dense clouds aloft? Elihu's answer is theological: divine skill (tĕmîm dēʿôṯ, 'perfect in knowledge', the same epithet Elihu applied to himself in Job 36:4). The rhetorical question form anticipates Job 38's relentless 'can you...? do you know...?' interrogation.
17: 'Why your clothes are warm when the earth lies still under the south wind?' — the question about bodily warmth and the stilling of the wind during hot weather. The theological point: even something as immediate and personal as the warmth of one's clothing is connected to divine governance of the atmosphere. The south wind (dārôm) bringing heat appears in Luke 12:55 ('when you see the south wind blowing, you say it will be hot'). The mundane experience of weather on the skin is one more point in the continuum of divine care.
18: 'Have you joined him in spreading out the sky (rāqîaʿ), firm and hard as a poured metal mirror?' — rāqaʿ (to spread out by hammering; rāqîaʿ = the firmament of Genesis 1:6-8) applied to the sky as a hammered-metal surface. The 'poured metal mirror' (kĕmareʾāh mûṣāq) is a polished bronze mirror — the same technology as the bronze mirrors donated by the women at the Tabernacle (Exodus 38:8). The firmament-as-mirror metaphor suggests the sky as the polished surface that reflects divine glory downward. Paul may echo this in 1 Corinthians 13:12 ('now we see through a mirror dimly').
19: 'Tell us what we should say to him — we cannot arrange our words because of our darkness' — the epistemological admission of limit: human darkness (ḥōšeḵ, the same word for pre-creation chaos in Genesis 1:2) prevents the right arrangement of words before God. The irony is that Elihu has been arranging extensive words to Job on God's behalf; now he acknowledges the limit of human speech before the divine. This admission is the threshold before theophany: the collapse of human language precedes the divine word.
20: 'Should he be told that I want to speak? Can anyone speak to him without being consumed?' — the question of whether human speech registers with God at all. The consumption (yĕbullāʿ, from bālaʿ = to swallow up) is the fate of one who approaches God without proper mediation — the same concern as Exodus 33:20 ('you cannot see my face, for no one can see me and live'). Elihu's rhetoric has reached its limit: the speech about God's speech has led to silence before the actual divine presence.
21: 'And now people cannot look at the bright light (ʾôr bāhîr) in the clouds; but the wind comes and sweeps them away' — the inaccessibility of the divine light in the clouds: human eyes cannot look directly at the luminous theophanic cloud-presence. The bright light (bāhîr, = pure/clear/shining) in clouds connects to the Sinai theophany (Exodus 24:16-17 'the glory of the LORD looked like consuming fire') and Ezekiel's chariot-vision (Ezekiel 1:4, 27-28). The wind that sweeps the obscuring clouds away prepares for the wind/whirlwind from which God will actually speak in v1 of Job 38.
22: 'From the north comes golden brightness (mizzāhāḇ yāʾāteh); God comes in fearsome majesty (nôrāʾ hôḏ)' — the golden light from the north: Psalms 48:2 identifies Zion as 'beautiful in elevation, the joy of the whole earth... in the far north (yarkĕṯê ṣāp̄ôn).' The Ugaritic texts describe Baal's palace as located on the holy mountain in the north (Ṣāp̄ôn = Zaphon/the north). Elihu uses the same sacred-geography vocabulary for YHWH: the gold-brightness from the north is YHWH's own theophanic approach. hôḏ (majesty/splendor) is the divine attribute of Psalms 96:6; Job 104:1; 1 Chronicles 29:11 (David's doxology).
23: 'As for the Almighty (Šaddai), we cannot reach him (lōʾ-nimmĕṣāʾhû); he is great in power (ḵōaḥ), in justice (mišpāṭ), and in abundant righteousness (ṣĕḏāqāh)' — the triple attribute of divine greatness is the closest Elihu comes to a formal doxology. The pairing of power with justice and righteousness prevents the divine might from being raw force: it is morally structured power. This framing anticipates the divine speech, which will demonstrate power but will be implicitly just in vindicating Job. Paul draws on Job 23:3 and this language in Romans 11:33 ('the depth of the riches... of the knowledge of God; how unsearchable his judgments and his paths beyond tracing').
24: 'Therefore people fear him; he does not attend to those who are wise in their own eyes (ḥaḵmê-lēḇ)' — the conclusion of Elihu's speech: appropriate fear of God is the human response to the incomprehensibility-and-righteousness of divine majesty. The final clause — that God does not attend to those wise in their own eyes — echoes Proverbs 3:7 ('do not be wise in your own eyes; fear the LORD') and functions as a subtle final critique of Job's confidence in his own case: self-assured wisdom before God is precisely the posture that forfeits divine attention. The irony is that Elihu himself has displayed this same self-assured wisdom throughout Job 32-37.
